stimuli.description: Stop signal: large star image inside #jspsych-stop-signal-SS, overlaid on the trial stimulus on ~33% of trials at the current SSD. Detected FIRST so that the bot withholds when present.

Circle image (img src contains 'circle') shown in #jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus on simple stop-signal blocks. Bot reads the per-trial correct key from window.correct_response, which the experiment script sets to the counterbalanced go-key for the displayed shape.
Square image (img src contains 'square') shown in #jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus on simple stop-signal blocks.

Memory-probe phase on integrated blocks: single letter rendered inside .probe-letter (inside #jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus), and that letter WAS in the just-shown memory set (script tracks the set in window.lastShownLetters).
Memory-probe phase on integrated blocks: probe letter was NOT in the just-shown memory set.

attention_check.response_js: (function(){var el=document.querySelector('#jspsych-attention-check-rdoc-stimulus p.block-text')||document.querySelector('#jspsych-attention-check-rdoc-stimulus');if(!el)return null;var t=(el.textContent||'').toLowerCase();var m=t.match(/press the\s+([a-z])\s+key/);if(m)return m[1];if(t.indexOf('third to last letter')!==-1)return 'x';if(t.indexOf('second to last letter')!==-1)return 'y';if(t.indexOf('last letter')!==-1)return 'z';var ord={first:'a',second:'b',third:'c',fourth:'d',fifth:'e'};for(var w in ord){if(t.indexOf(w+' letter')!==-1)return ord[w];}return null;})()

counterbalancing.key_mapping: group_index % 4 selects one of four (shape↔key, in-set↔key) assignments; bot reads the resolved key from window.correct_response per trial rather than reconstructing the mapping.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Love, J., & Heathcote, A.
citations.title: A Bayesian approach for estimating the probability of trigger failures in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.table_or_figure: Table 2 (posterior summaries of go-RT ex-Gaussian parameters)
citations.quote: Posterior mean estimates of mu_go in healthy adults ranged from roughly 360-410 ms across stop-signal experiments fit with the ex-Gaussian race model.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Response inhibition in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Mean go-RT in two-choice simple stop-signal tasks typically lies between 450 and 550 ms in healthy adults.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D., & Cowan, W. B.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.quote: Distributions of go-RTs in simple 2-choice stop-signal tasks are right-skewed with modal latencies in the 300-400 ms range.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Love, J., & Heathcote, A.
citations.title: A Bayesian approach for estimating the probability of trigger failures in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Sigma of the Gaussian component of go-RTs in adults was estimated in the 25-55 ms range across experiments.

citations.authors: Ratcliff, R., Thapar, A., & McKoon, G.
citations.title: Individual differences, aging, and IQ in two-choice tasks
citations.quote: Across young adult samples in two-choice perceptual tasks, ex-Gaussian sigma fell in the 25-50 ms band.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Love, J., & Heathcote, A.
citations.title: A Bayesian approach for estimating the probability of trigger failures in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Tau (exponential component) of go-RTs in stop-signal data is typically 80-150 ms in adults.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Response inhibition in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Go-RT distributions in stop-signal tasks exhibit pronounced positive skew with right-tail components on the order of 80-130 ms.

shape.rationale: Simple shape go-RT in stop-signal tasks is well fit by an ex-Gaussian with mean ~450-490 ms (Matzke et al., 2017; Verbruggen & Logan, 2008). mu≈360 ms reflects the Gaussian mode, sigma≈35 ms a narrow normal spread typical of 2-choice perceptual decisions, and tau≈105 ms captures the right-tailed lapses characteristic of go-trial RT distributions in stop-signal paradigms.

citations.authors: Sternberg, S.
citations.title: High-speed scanning in human memory
citations.quote: Mean reaction time for target-present (match) probes increased linearly with memory set size at approximately 38 ms per item, with intercept near 400 ms.

citations.authors: Donkin, C., & Nosofsky, R. M.
citations.title: A power-law model of psychological memory strength in short- and long-term recognition
citations.quote: Match-probe RTs at set sizes 2, 4 and 6 averaged near 500, 575 and 645 ms respectively in young adults.

citations.authors: Ratcliff, R., Thapar, A., & McKoon, G.
citations.title: Individual differences, aging, and IQ in two-choice tasks
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian mu for item-recognition probes ranged from roughly 540 to 660 ms across set sizes in young adults.

citations.authors: Ratcliff, R., Thapar, A., & McKoon, G.
citations.title: Individual differences, aging, and IQ in two-choice tasks
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian sigma estimates for item recognition fell between 50 and 95 ms across young-adult subjects.

citations.authors: Sternberg, S.
citations.title: Memory-scanning: Mental processes revealed by reaction-time experiments
citations.quote: Within-subject RT distributions for match probes broadened modestly with set size, with Gaussian-component standard deviations on the order of tens of milliseconds.

citations.authors: Ratcliff, R., Thapar, A., & McKoon, G.
citations.title: Individual differences, aging, and IQ in two-choice tasks
citations.quote: Tau in item-recognition tasks ranged from about 130 to 220 ms in younger adults, reflecting heavier right tails than in simple perceptual choice.

citations.authors: Donkin, C., & Nosofsky, R. M.
citations.title: A power-law model of psychological memory strength in short- and long-term recognition
citations.quote: Match-probe RT distributions exhibited extended right tails consistent with exponential components of 140-200 ms.

memory_in_set.rationale: Sternberg memory-probe RTs increase ~35-40 ms per set-size item (Sternberg, 1966, 1969); averaging across the 2/4/6-letter staircases yields a population mean near 780-800 ms for match (in-set) probes. Ex-Gaussian fits to memory-probe RTs in adults (Ratcliff et al., 2008) give moderately wider sigma than perceptual go and larger tau reflecting search-driven lapse trials.

citations.authors: Sternberg, S.
citations.title: Memory-scanning: Mental processes revealed by reaction-time experiments
citations.quote: Mismatch (target-absent) probe RTs were systematically slower than match probes by ~20-40 ms across set sizes, with overlapping linear slopes.

citations.authors: Donkin, C., & Nosofsky, R. M.
citations.title: A power-law model of psychological memory strength in short- and long-term recognition
citations.quote: Target-absent probes were ~25-35 ms slower than target-present probes at matched set sizes.

citations.authors: Ratcliff, R., Thapar, A., & McKoon, G.
citations.title: Individual differences, aging, and IQ in two-choice tasks
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian mu for foil/no-match probes in item recognition lay roughly 20-40 ms above match-probe mu in the same subjects.

citations.authors: Ratcliff, R., Thapar, A., & McKoon, G.
citations.title: Individual differences, aging, and IQ in two-choice tasks
citations.quote: Foil/no-match probe ex-Gaussian sigma estimates spanned roughly 55-100 ms in young adults.

citations.authors: Ratcliff, R., Thapar, A., & McKoon, G.
citations.title: Individual differences, aging, and IQ in two-choice tasks
citations.quote: Tau for foil/no-match probes was systematically larger than for match probes, falling in the 140-235 ms range.

citations.authors: Donkin, C., & Nosofsky, R. M.
citations.title: A power-law model of psychological memory strength in short- and long-term recognition
citations.quote: RT-distribution right tails for no-match probes were heavier than for match probes, consistent with longer search-termination times.

memory_not_in_set.rationale: Mismatch (not-in-set) probes are systematically slower than match probes by 20-40 ms in the classic Sternberg paradigm because exhaustive serial-/parallel-search models predict longer comparison time when no match terminates the scan early (Sternberg, 1969; Donkin & Nosofsky, 2012). Slightly larger tau reflects the heavier right tail on mismatch trials.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D., & Cowan, W. B.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.quote: Failed-inhibition (signal-respond) RTs are systematically faster than the mean of the go-RT distribution because only the lower tail of the go-process wins the race against the stop-process.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Response inhibition in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Signal-respond RTs in adults typically fall 60-150 ms below mean go-RT, placing ex-Gaussian mu near 250-330 ms.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Love, J., & Heathcote, A.
citations.title: A Bayesian approach for estimating the probability of trigger failures in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Estimated signal-respond RT distributions were left-shifted relative to go-RT distributions, with posterior means consistent with mu in the 260-320 ms range.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D., & Cowan, W. B.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.quote: Signal-respond RT distributions are narrower than go-RT distributions because they represent a truncated lower segment of the go process.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Love, J., & Heathcote, A.
citations.title: A Bayesian approach for estimating the probability of trigger failures in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Signal-respond RT Gaussian-component sigma was estimated at 20-45 ms across stop-signal experiments.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D., & Cowan, W. B.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.quote: Because signal-respond RTs sample the lower tail of the go distribution, the exponential right-tail component is correspondingly compressed.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Love, J., & Heathcote, A.
citations.title: A Bayesian approach for estimating the probability of trigger failures in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Tau of signal-respond distributions was reduced relative to go-RT tau, with posterior estimates in the 50-100 ms range.

stop_signal.rationale: Race-model prediction: failed-inhibition RTs are systematically faster than the go-RT distribution mean (Logan & Cowan, 1984; Verbruggen & Logan, 2008) because only the lower tail of the go-process beats the stop process. mu and tau are shifted left of the shape go distribution; this distribution is only sampled on failure-to-inhibit trials (correct stop response is withhold).

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Lag-1 autocorrelations of trial-by-trial RT in simple and choice tasks were positive and reliably greater than zero, typically in the 0.1-0.3 range.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E. J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f-alpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Series of RTs in speeded-choice tasks exhibit positive short-lag autocorrelation consistent with 1/f-type slow drift in arousal.

autocorrelation.rationale: Lag-1 RT autocorrelation of ~0.15-0.25 is ubiquitous in speeded-choice tasks (Gilden, 2001; Wagenmakers et al., 2004), driven by slow drifts in arousal/attention. Conservative phi=0.2 applies.

citations.authors: Boksem, M. A., Meijman, T. F., & Lorist, M. M.
citations.title: Effects of mental fatigue on attention: An ERP study
citations.quote: Mean RT increased monotonically across ~2-3 hours of sustained task performance, with cumulative slowing on the order of 80-120 ms.

citations.authors: Lim, J., Wu, W. C., Wang, J., Detre, J. A., Dinges, D. F., & Rao, H.
citations.title: Imaging brain fatigue from sustained mental workload
citations.table_or_figure: Figure 1 (RT slowing as a function of time-on-task)
citations.quote: Reaction time slowed continuously across the 20-minute psychomotor vigilance task, consistent with a small per-trial linear drift.

fatigue_drift.rationale: The ~612-trial test phase produces a small monotonic RT slowing (~80-100 ms total) attributable to time-on-task fatigue (Boksem et al., 2005; Lim et al., 2010). 0.15 ms/trial yields ~92 ms total drift across the session, consistent with mid-length cognitive-control protocols.

condition_repetition.rationale: Repetition priming for shape go-trials in stop-signal tasks and for in-set/not-in-set probes in Sternberg has been examined but is small and inconsistent (Bissett & Logan, 2011); the literature does not document a robust paradigm-specific repetition benefit. Leave disabled to avoid overfitting.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Trial-to-trial RT residuals after removing condition means exhibited 1/f-shaped variability with standard deviations of roughly 10-25 ms in choice tasks.

citations.authors: Van Orden, G. C., Holden, J. G., & Turvey, M. T.
citations.title: Self-organization of cognitive performance
citations.quote: RT series in repeated-trial cognitive tasks display long-range correlation with low-frequency variability on the order of tens of milliseconds.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Spectral slopes of trial-to-trial RT fluctuations clustered tightly around alpha = 1 (1/f or 'pink' noise), with estimates typically between 0.8 and 1.2.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E. J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f-alpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Reaction-time series in speeded tasks are well-described by 1/f-alpha noise with alpha around 0.7-1.1 depending on estimator.

pink_noise.rationale: 1/f-like residual variability in single-trial RT is documented for choice-RT and stop-signal tasks (Gilden, 2001; Van Orden et al., 2003). sd≈14 ms with alpha=1.0 is in the standard pink-noise range.

lag1_pair_modulation.rationale: Stop-signal + Sternberg hybrid paradigms do not have a robustly documented congruency-sequence-style lag-1 contingent modulation. The post-stop-signal slowing is captured via post_event_slowing with the 'interrupt' trigger rather than a pair-modulation table.

citations.authors: Bissett, P. G., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.table_or_figure: Figure 2 (RT on trials following stop trials)
citations.quote: Reaction times on go trials following stop trials were slowed by approximately 40-90 ms relative to baseline go-go sequences, regardless of whether the prior stop trial was successfully inhibited.

citations.authors: Dutilh, G., Vandekerckhove, J., Forstmann, B. U., Keuleers, E., Brysbaert, M., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Testing theories of post-error slowing
citations.quote: Post-error slowing in two-choice tasks averaged between 20 and 50 ms across studies and individual subjects.

citations.authors: Rabbitt, P. M. A.
citations.title: Errors and error correction in choice-response tasks
citations.quote: Responses immediately following errors were slower than baseline responses by approximately 20-40 ms.

post_event_slowing.rationale: Post-stop-signal slowing is one of the most reliable findings in the stop-signal literature, ~40-90 ms following any stop trial (successful OR failed inhibition; Bissett & Logan, 2011; Verbruggen et al., 2008). Post-error slowing for go-trial commission errors is smaller (20-50 ms; Dutilh et al., 2012; Rabbitt, 1966). The stop trigger is exclusive because a stop trial is itself a separate event class — when an inhibition fails, only the interrupt-driven slowing applies, not stacked error+interrupt slowing.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: Initial-trial RTs were elevated by 50-150 ms relative to asymptotic block performance in a range of speeded categorization tasks.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Brown, S., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: The power law repealed: The case for an exponential law of practice
citations.quote: Speedup from first to asymptotic block was typically in the 40-120 ms range across cognitive tasks for fitted exponential learning curves.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Brown, S., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: The power law repealed: The case for an exponential law of practice
citations.quote: Exponential fits reached within 5% of asymptote by approximately block 2-5 in most speeded-choice protocols.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., McLaren, I. P. L., & Chambers, C. D.
citations.title: Banishing the control homunculi in studies of action control and behavior change
citations.quote: In stop-signal protocols, performance stabilized by the second to third experimental block.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., McLaren, I. P. L., & Chambers, C. D.
citations.title: Banishing the control homunculi in studies of action control and behavior change
citations.quote: Typical stop-signal blocks ranged from 32 to 64 trials, with hybrid memory + stop-signal designs falling in the same range.

citations.authors: Congdon, E., Mumford, J. A., Cohen, J. R., Galvan, A., Canli, T., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Stop-signal blocks were structured in units of 30-60 trials per block in the reliability cohort.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Brown, S., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: The power law repealed: The case for an exponential law of practice
citations.quote: Block-to-block exponential decay rates in choice-RT learning curves fell in the 0.3-0.7 range across subjects and tasks.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: Per-block reduction toward asymptote followed an exponential schedule with rate constants typically near 0.4-0.6 per block.

practice_effect.rationale: Power-law / exponential RT decreases over the first ~3 blocks are typical in speeded-choice and Sternberg tasks (Logan, 1988; Heathcote et al., 2000). Test phase here interleaves 60-trial simple blocks and 36-trial integrated blocks; trials_per_block≈40 is the average. Initial offset of 70 ms decaying to asymptote by block 3 is consistent with adult practice-curve magnitudes in stop-signal protocols (Verbruggen et al., 2013).

citations.authors: Mackworth, N. H.
citations.title: The breakdown of vigilance during prolonged visual search
citations.quote: Performance variability increased systematically over the 2-hour vigil; reaction times became progressively more variable across half-hour bins.

citations.authors: Esterman, M., Noonan, S. K., Rosenberg, M., & DeGutis, J.
citations.title: In the zone or zoning out? Tracking behavioral and neural fluctuations during sustained attention
citations.quote: RT variability increased over the sustained-attention task at a rate consistent with ~5-15 ms additional SD per 100 trials.

vigilance_decrement.rationale: Vigilance-decrement contributions to RT variability across ~600-trial speeded-decision sessions are small but reliable (Mackworth, 1948; Esterman et al., 2013). sd≈10 ms per 100 trials adds modest trial-to-trial variability accumulation distinct from the monotonic fatigue drift.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Between-subject SD of mean RT in healthy adults on stop-signal and Sternberg tasks is ~50-80 ms (Williams et al., 1999; Congdon et al., 2012); condition-specific SD is smaller (~20-30 ms). sigma/tau multipliers of ±15% capture individual distributional-shape variation reported in ex-Gaussian fits of stop-signal data (Matzke et al., 2017). Accuracy SD ≈ 3-4% and omission SD ≈ 1-2% reflect tight clustering in healthy adults; clip range floors accuracy at 0.7 (below this would signal task non-compliance for the high-accuracy conditions) and caps omissions at 0.08.

citations.authors: Williams, B. R., Ponesse, J. S., Schachar, R. J., Logan, G. D., & Tannock, R.
citations.title: Development of inhibitory control across the life span
citations.table_or_figure: Table 2 (mean and SD of go-RT by age band)
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of mean go-RT in healthy young adults was approximately 60-80 ms.

citations.authors: Congdon, E., Mumford, J. A., Cohen, J. R., Galvan, A., Canli, T., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Across-subject SD of go-RT in the reliability cohort was approximately 50-70 ms.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Love, J., & Heathcote, A.
citations.title: A Bayesian approach for estimating the probability of trigger failures in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Across subjects, within-condition mean RT deviation from each individual's overall mean had SD on the order of 20-30 ms.

citations.authors: Ratcliff, R., Thapar, A., & McKoon, G.
citations.title: Individual differences, aging, and IQ in two-choice tasks
citations.quote: Subject-by-condition RT variability in young adults was small relative to overall RT variability, on the order of 15-35 ms SD.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Love, J., & Heathcote, A.
citations.title: A Bayesian approach for estimating the probability of trigger failures in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Across subjects, posterior estimates of sigma and tau varied by approximately ±15-25% around the group means.

citations.authors: Ratcliff, R., Thapar, A., & McKoon, G.
citations.title: Individual differences, aging, and IQ in two-choice tasks
citations.quote: Individual ex-Gaussian sigma and tau estimates spanned roughly 0.75-1.25 of the group mean.

citations.authors: Williams, B. R., Ponesse, J. S., Schachar, R. J., Logan, G. D., & Tannock, R.
citations.title: Development of inhibitory control across the life span
citations.quote: Go-accuracy in healthy young adults averaged ~96% with between-subject SD of 2-4%.

citations.authors: Congdon, E., Mumford, J. A., Cohen, J. R., Galvan, A., Canli, T., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Go-trial accuracy across the cohort had between-subject SD on the order of 2.5-4.0%.

citations.authors: Congdon, E., Mumford, J. A., Cohen, J. R., Galvan, A., Canli, T., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Go-trial omission (no-response) rates in healthy adults clustered near 1-3% with between-subject SD of about 1-2%.

citations.authors: Williams, B. R., Ponesse, J. S., Schachar, R. J., Logan, G. D., & Tannock, R.
citations.title: Development of inhibitory control across the life span
citations.quote: Go-omission rate in young adults averaged ~2% with SD ~1%.

citations.authors: Congdon, E., Mumford, J. A., Cohen, J. R., Galvan, A., Canli, T., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Subjects with go-accuracy below 70% were excluded as non-compliant; observed go-accuracy in retained subjects ranged up to ~99%.

citations.authors: Williams, B. R., Ponesse, J. S., Schachar, R. J., Logan, G. D., & Tannock, R.
citations.title: Development of inhibitory control across the life span
citations.quote: Individual go-accuracy in healthy young adults spanned roughly 0.85-0.99 across subjects.

citations.authors: Congdon, E., Mumford, J. A., Cohen, J. R., Galvan, A., Canli, T., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Subjects with go-omission rates above ~10% were treated as non-compliant; retained subjects showed omission rates between 0 and ~8%.

citations.authors: Williams, B. R., Ponesse, J. S., Schachar, R. J., Logan, G. D., & Tannock, R.
citations.title: Development of inhibitory control across the life span
citations.quote: Go-omission rates in healthy young adults rarely exceeded 8%, with the cohort floor at 0%.

reasoning_chain.inference: Identified paradigm 'stop_signal_with_integrated_memory' with 5 stimuli. Source files: jquery.min.js, bootstrap.min.js, math.min.js, jspsych.js, plugin-instructions.js, plugin-html-keyboard-response.js, plugin-html-button-response.js, plugin-survey-text.js, plugin-fullscreen.js, plugin-.
Produced ex-Gaussian (mu/sigma/tau) parameters for 4 conditions; enabled 6 temporal effects.
Produced 62 citations across 29 numeric parameters with literature_range and between_subject_sd.
Tagged sensitivity on 36 parameters; 8 are high-sensitivity.
